title: IRR and NPV Investment Calculator 2026 | Hacé Cuentas
h1: Calculate IRR and NPV for Investment Projects
description: Evaluate investments with IRR, NPV, and payback analysis. Compare against opportunity cost—savings accounts, bonds, WACC. Make better investment decisions.
intro: You have **$10 million** and someone offers a project that returns **$3M per year for 5 years**. Should you take it or leave the money in a savings account earning 40%? Two classic financial metrics answer this: **NPV (Net Present Value)** and **IRR (Internal Rate of Return)**. The **NPV** tells you how much **wealth in today's dollars** the project creates after accounting for your opportunity cost. The **IRR** tells you the **project's effective return**, which you can directly compare to your best alternative (savings account, bonds, another business). This calculator takes your **initial investment**, **expected annual cash flow**, **project duration**, and **discount rate** (opportunity cost), then returns **NPV, IRR, payback period, total cash received**, and an **automatic verdict**. Used in **business decisions** (CapEx, expansions, new products), **real estate investing** (does rental income beat a bond?), **equipment evaluation**, **personal projects** (franchise, online course, crypto staking). 2026 rates — customize to your situation.
keyTakeaway: **NPV = −Initial Investment + Σ Cash Flow / (1+r)^t**. If **NPV > 0** → project creates value. **IRR = discount rate r* that makes NPV = 0**. Rule: **accept if IRR > discount rate**. **Payback** = years to recover investment without discounting.
useCases: Decide whether **rental income from an apartment** beats a sovereign bond.
Your company analyzes **expansion to a new city** with CapEx of $50M.
You're deciding between **buying a franchise** or investing in a diversified portfolio.
Compare **two industrial machines** with different costs and useful lives.
Evaluate **crypto staking or liquidity pools** against a savings account.

fields.label: Annual cash flow
fields.help: If cash flows vary by year, use the average. For uneven cash flows, build a detailed schedule.

fields.label: Project duration (years)
fields.help: Length of the project in years.

fields.label: Discount rate (opportunity cost)
fields.help: Minimum required return: savings account, bond yield, or your company's WACC.

dataUpdate.notes: IRR and NPV: pure financial formulas based on user-provided cash flows.

example.title: Example: $10M investment, $3M annual cash flow for 5 years, 40% discount rate
steps: **Discount rate**: 40% annually (your opportunity cost, savings account).
example.result: NPV **negative (−$3.89M)** and IRR **15.2% < discount rate 40%**: the project **doesn't pay**. If you lowered your discount rate to 15%, NPV would be +$56,700 (marginal). **Verdict**: keep the money in savings at 40% — higher return with no risk.

explanation:
## What are NPV and IRR

They're the **two classic indicators** for project evaluation, found in every corporate finance textbook (Brealey-Myers, Ross, Damodaran).

### Net Present Value (NPV)

It's the **sum of discounted future cash flows** in today's dollars, minus the initial investment:

```
NPV = −Investment + Σ (Cash Flow_t / (1+r)^t)   for t = 1 to n
```

- **NPV > 0**: project generates more value than your required return → **ACCEPT**.
- **NPV = 0**: indifferent (project returns exactly your required rate).
- **NPV < 0**: project destroys value → **REJECT**.

### Internal Rate of Return (IRR)

It's the **discount rate r* that makes NPV = 0**. It represents the **project's effective return rate**.

```
0 = −Investment + Σ (Cash Flow_t / (1+r*)^t)
```

No closed-form solution — calculated iteratively (Newton-Raphson).

- **IRR > discount rate** → project returns more than your opportunity cost → **ACCEPT**.
- **IRR < discount rate** → returns less → **REJECT**.

### Payback Period

Years needed to **recover the investment without discounting**. Useful but secondary (ignores time value of money).

```
Simple payback = Investment / Annual cash flow
```

## Decision Table

| NPV | IRR vs Discount Rate | Decision |
|---|---|---|
| > 0 | IRR > r | **ACCEPT** (aligned signals) |
| = 0 | IRR = r | Indifferent |
| < 0 | IRR < r | **REJECT** (aligned signals) |
| > 0 | IRR < r | Rare — complex flows, recalculate |
| < 0 | IRR > r | Rare — complex flows, recalculate |

For projects with **all positive cash flows after initial investment**, NPV and IRR always align. With **mixed cash flows** (positive and negative), there can be **multiple IRRs** — trust NPV only in those cases.

## Typical Discount Rates (Argentina 2026)

| Source | Rate (approx 2026) | When to use |
|---|---|---|
| **Savings account** | 0-5% | Never (inflation erodes gains) |
| **Fixed deposit (traditional)** | 35-45% APR | Personal investment decisions |
| **Fixed deposit (inflation-linked)** | CER + 1% (real) | Long-term real returns |
| **Inflation-linked bond** | CER + 5-8% | Market benchmark |
| **Short-term instrument (LECAP)** | 35-50% APR | Short-term opportunity cost |
| **Argentine USD bond** | 10-20% USD | USD-denominated projects |
| **US Treasury 10Y** | 4-5% USD | Low-volatility USD projects |
| **WACC (SME Argentina)** | 25-40% | Corporate projects |
| **Private equity** | 30-50% real | Venture capital |

For **high-risk projects** (new venture, innovation), add a **risk premium** of 5-15 percentage points above the base rate.

## Example 1: Apartment Rental

**Scenario**: Buy apartment for **USD 100,000**. Rents for **USD 500/month** = USD 6,000/year. Hold 10 years, sell for USD 110,000.

```
Initial investment: −USD 100,000
Cash flows (years 1-9): USD 6,000
Cash flow (year 10): USD 6,000 + USD 110,000 = USD 116,000
Discount rate: 7% USD (Argentine USD bonds)

NPV = −100,000 + (6,000 × 7.02) + (110,000 / 1.07^10)
    = −100,000 + 42,120 + 55,914
    = −$1,966 USD (slightly negative)
IRR ≈ 6.7% USD
```

**Verdict**: returns **less than a USD bond** (not financially compelling — though the property offers emotional value, real appreciation, etc.).

## Example 2: Franchise

**Scenario**: Cafe franchise. Initial investment **$50M**. Net cash flow **$15M/year** for 5 years (contract term). Discount rate 40% (savings account + expansion alternative).

```
NPV = −50M + 15M × [1 − 1.40^(−5)] / 0.40
    = −50M + 15M × 2.035
    = −50M + 30.53M
    = −$19.47M (negative)
IRR ≈ 15.2% (well below 40%)
```

**Verdict**: **Not worth it** at 40% opportunity cost. Only works if your discount rate falls below 15%, which is rare in Argentina.

## NPV and IRR with Variable Cash Flows

This calculator assumes **constant annual cash flow**. For variable flows (year 1 = $2M, year 2 = $5M, year 3 = $8M...), use **Excel or Google Sheets** with:

- `=NPV(rate; cash_flows)` or `=VNA()` (Spanish)
- `=IRR(cash_flows)` or `=TIR()` (Spanish)

## Why IRR Can Mislead

1. **Different project scales**: High IRR on a small project doesn't beat lower IRR on a large one. **NPV wins**.
2. **Multiple IRRs**: If cash flows flip signs multiple times, multiple IRRs exist. Use **NPV only**.
3. **Reinvestment assumption**: IRR assumes you reinvest intermediate cash flows at the same IRR rate. If your IRR is 80%, that's unrealistic — **Modified IRR (MIRR)** corrects this.
4. **Mutually exclusive projects**: Can only choose A or B, not both? Pick **highest NPV**, not highest IRR.

## Discounted Payback

Standard payback ignores the time value of money. **Discounted payback** applies a discount rate:

| Year | Cash Flow | Present Value (40%) | Cumulative |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | −10,000,000 | −10,000,000 | −10,000,000 |
| 1 | 3,000,000 | 2,142,857 | −7,857,143 |
| 2 | 3,000,000 | 1,530,612 | −6,326,531 |
| 3 | 3,000,000 | 1,093,294 | −5,233,237 |
| 4 | 3,000,000 | 780,924 | −4,452,313 |
| 5 | 3,000,000 | 557,803 | −3,894,510 |

Discounted payback: **never recovers** (at 40% rate).
Simple payback: **3.33 years**.

## WACC — Corporate Discount Rate

For companies, use the **WACC (Weighted Average Cost of Capital)**:

```
WACC = (E/V × Re) + (D/V × Rd × (1 − T))
```

Where:
- **E** = market value of equity
- **D** = market value of debt
- **V** = E + D (total value)
- **Re** = cost of equity (from CAPM)
- **Rd** = cost of debt
- **T** = corporate tax rate (25-35% Argentina)

**CAPM** calculates **Re = Rf + β × (Rm − Rf)**:
- **Rf** = risk-free rate (US Treasury 10Y ~4.5%)
- **β** = sector beta
- **Rm − Rf** = market risk premium (~6-8%)
- **+ Argentina country risk premium** (~6-10%)

For Argentine SMEs, WACC typically **25-40% in pesos** or **12-20% in USD**.

## Common Mistakes in NPV/IRR

1. **Wrong currency**: USD cash flows → USD rate. Peso flows → peso rate (includes inflation).
2. **Ignoring taxes**: Always use **after-tax cash flows**. For companies: **Net Flow = (Revenue − Costs − Depreciation) × (1 − Tax Rate) + Depreciation**. Depreciation isn't cash, but reduces taxable income.
3. **Missing salvage value**: When the project ends, do you sell the asset? Include that as a final cash flow.
4. **Ignoring working capital**: New projects need upfront investment in inventory, receivables. Don't forget!
5. **Overestimating cash flows**: Always project **pessimistic/base/optimistic** scenarios.
6. **Comparing IRRs across different time horizons**: Project A (3 years, 50% IRR) vs B (10 years, 30% IRR) — use **NPV** or **equivalent annual NPV** for fair comparison.

## Related Calculators

- [Compound Interest](/calculadora-interes-compuesto) — the foundation of discounting.
- [Fixed Deposit](/calculadora-plazo-fijo) — typical opportunity cost.
- [Loan Payment](/calculadora-cuota-prestamo) — the flip side of investment.
- [Inflation Adjustment](/calculadora-actualizacion-inflacion-ipc) — for real projections.
- [Business Break-Even](/calculadora-facturacion-minima-negocio) — profitability analysis.

faq.q: What discount rate should I use for my analysis?
faq.a: Use **your opportunity cost** — the best alternative use of that money. Options: **fixed deposit APR** (35-45% 2026), **inflation-linked bond** (CER+5-8%), **LECAP**, or a **competing project** you know. For companies, use **WACC**. If your project is **riskier** than the benchmark, add a **risk premium** (5-15 points). Rule: **discount rate ≥ risk-free return + premium**. For USD projects, use 4-8% USD rates.

faq.q: What's the difference between NPV and IRR?
faq.a: **NPV** measures **absolute wealth** (dollars today), **IRR** measures **percentage return**. Example: Project A invests $1M with NPV +$500K (IRR 40%). Project B invests $10M with NPV +$2M (IRR 25%). **Choose B by NPV** (more dollars), **choose A by IRR** (higher percentage). Rule: when choosing between two projects, **maximize NPV**. Use IRR to check if returns beat your minimum threshold.

faq.q: Why is my IRR negative or extremely high?
faq.a: **IRR very high**: either (1) genuinely excellent project, (2) cash flows overestimated, (3) investment underestimated. **IRR negative**: your cash flows never recover the initial investment, even with 0% discount rate. Check: (1) units (pesos vs USD?), (2) time period, (3) sign of each cash flow (negative for outflows). **Multiple IRRs**: if flows flip between positive/negative multiple times, multiple solutions exist — trust **NPV only** in that case.

faq.q: How do I handle variable cash flows year by year?
faq.a: This calculator assumes **constant annual flow**. For variable flows: **(1)** use the **average** for a quick estimate; **(2)** build a **spreadsheet with actual flows** and use `=NPV(rate; flows)` + `=IRR(flows)`; **(3)** for flows growing at constant rate, use the **Gordon model**: NPV = F / (r − g) where g is growth rate. Always add the **terminal value** (sale of asset) as the final cash flow.

faq.q: Is a small positive NPV enough to justify investing?
faq.a: **It depends on risk and effort**. NPV of +$50K on $10M investment (0.5%) is basically **indifferent** — any surprise wipes it out. **Best practice**: demand **safety margin** of NPV/Investment ≥ 20-30%. Also model a **downside scenario** (flows down 30%) — does NPV stay positive? Projects worth doing have clearly positive NPV and survive shocks.

faq.q: What happens if my discount rate changes?
faq.a: NPV is **very sensitive** to the discount rate. **Lower rates** → present value of future flows increases → **NPV rises**. **Higher rates** → future flows are discounted more → **NPV falls**. The **IRR is the breakeven point**: below IRR, NPV is positive; above IRR, it's negative. **Sensitivity analysis**: recalculate NPV at rate ± 5 percentage points to stress-test your decision. Crucial in volatile Argentina markets.

faq.q: Can I have a high IRR but low NPV?
faq.a: **Yes, in small-scale projects**. Example: invest $100K, receive $200K in 1 year = **IRR 100%** but **NPV only +$33K** (at 50% discount rate). **Very profitable %** but small absolute dollars. Fine for personal decisions or small ventures. For corporations optimizing **shareholder wealth**, higher NPV wins (even if IRR is lower). It depends on your capital availability and whether you can replicate the project multiple times.

faq.q: How do I account for taxes in my analysis?
faq.a: Always use **after-tax cash flows**. For companies: **Net Flow = (Revenue − Operating Costs − Depreciation) × (1 − Tax Rate) + Depreciation**. Depreciation isn't actual cash, but it reduces taxable income. For Argentina: Income Tax is 25-35% (progressive). Also consider: **Gross Revenue Tax** (varies by province), **VAT** (IVA, 21%), **Asset Taxes** (Bienes Personales if applicable). For simplicity, many projects assume 30% effective tax. [Calculate your tax bracket](/calculadora-ganancias-tramos-empleado-mensual-2026).

faq.q: Should I include inflation in my cash flow projections?
faq.a: **Yes, always**. There are two approaches: **(1) Nominal**: project cash flows in inflation-adjusted pesos, use a **nominal discount rate** (which includes expected inflation); **(2) Real**: project cash flows in constant purchasing power, use a **real discount rate** (inflation-removed). Most people use **(1) nominal** by default in Argentina. If you use inflation-linked rates (CER), consistency is key. Argentina's 2026 inflation is volatile — model pessimistic/optimistic scenarios.

faq.q: How do I compare projects with different time horizons?
faq.a: Don't just compare raw **IRR** or **NPV** — they're biased toward different timeframes. **Solution**: calculate the **Equivalent Annual NPV** (NPV spread over years via annuity formula). Example: Project A (5 years, NPV $100K) vs Project B (10 years, NPV $150K). Convert both to an annual equivalent amount using discount rate to fairly compare. Or: replicate the shorter project over the same timeframe and recalculate NPV.
